markdown:
### Physical and Virtual Prototyping


#### The Purpose of Prototyping


1. **Validation**: Ensures the design meets user needs and functional requirements.
2. **Iteration**: Allows designers to refine and improve the product through feedback.
3. **Communication**: Serves as a tangible or visual representation to convey ideas to stakeholders.
4. **Risk Reduction**: Identifies potential issues early in the development process.

### Physical Prototyping


<definition term="Physical Prototype">A tangible, real-world model of a product, built using physical materials.</definition>

1. Used to test **form, ergonomics, weight, scale**, and real-world interaction.
2. Allow **hands-on testing **by users and stakeholders - tactile feedback.
3. Useful in evaluating **fit, movement**, or assembly of parts.
4. Often used in **later design stages **or when user interaction is critical.

![Clay modelling a car to evaluate its aesthetic appeal (Bove, 2023)](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/7c796cca44a1bf12e77c3fbb9c58b76b9ef1ae2e-1200x885.png)

### Virtual (Digital) Prototyping


<definition term="Virtual Prototypes">**Virtual prototypes** are digital models created using software tools like CAD (Computer-Aided Design) and simulation programs.</definition>

1. Useful for testing **geometry, motion, tolerance, and aesthetics.**
2. Can be used to run **simulations** (e.g. stress, airflow, heat distribution).
3. Easily shared, modified, and reused.
4. Common in **early development stages **and **cost-sensitive projects.**
5. Can be easily shared and reviewed by teams across different locations.

![Tesla Model S testing in a digital wind tunnel (Rus, 2015)](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/c8968170abc8673b5e3a1200572d9d833deb5cd8-1356x696.png)

<callout type="note">Many designers use a combination of physical and virtual prototyping to leverage the strengths of both methods.</callout>

| Feature | Physical Prototype | Virtual Prototype |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Form | Tangible, real object | Digital/simulated model |
| Test | Ergonomics, function, interaction | Structure, motion, forces, heat, etc. |
| Cost | Often higher (materials/tools) | Lower (but requires software & skills) |
| Iteration speed | Slower | Fast and easily editable |
| Best for | User testing, physical fit and feel | Early testing, digital collaboration, analysis |
